coredata.dc:title: Enzyme-free colorimetric detection of uric acid on the basis of MnO2 nanosheets - mediated oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′- tetramethylbenzidine
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Li
Qin, Shangying
Xu, Yuanjin
Cheng, Shiqi
Yang, Jin
Wang, Yilin
coredata.dc:description: MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) were prepared and used as both oxidant of 3, 3′, 5, 5′- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and recognizer of UA, a simple colorimetric approach for sensitive detection of uric acid (UA) was established. In the medium of weak acid, MnO2 NSs exhibited strong oxidation capacity and could oxidize colorless TMB to generate a blue oxidation product (oxTMB) with an absorbance signal. However, the introduction of UA could inhibit MnO2 NSs - mediated TMB oxidation and reduce the formation of oxTMB, resulting in the blue fading and the absorbance weakening. Under the conditions of incubation time 20 min, pH 3.0, TMB 0.5 mM, and MnO2 NSs 50 μM, the decrease in absorbance was linearly dependent on UA concentration in the range of 0.5–30.0 μM with a detection limit of 0.21 μM. Furthermore, the approach has been applied for determining UA in human urine samples with recoveries between 93.1 and 102.4 % and relative standard deviation less than 3.0 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MnO2 nanosheets
Uric acid
Colorimetry
Determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Linear response range and detection limit

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangxi key laboratory of electrochemical energy materials

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the opening project of Guangxi key laboratory of electrochemical energy materials (KF2020012) for its financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) Mn2+ and (b) MnO2 NSs; (B) FTIR and (C) XPS spectra of MnO2 NSs; (D) XPS spectrum of MnO2 NSs for Mn2p; (E) XRD pattern and (F) TEM image of MnO2 NSs.

(A) Absorption spectra of (a) TMB, (b) TMB + MnO2 NSs, (c) TMB + 10 μM UA + MnO2 NSs, (d) TMB + 20 μM UA + MnO2 NSs, (e) TMB + Mn2+; (B) Absorption spectra of (a) MnO2 NSs, (b) MnO2 NSs + 20 μM UA, (c) MnO2 NSs + 50 μM UA. Inset: the photos of corresponding solutions in sunlight. Experimental conditions: 50 μM MnO2 NSs, 0.5 mM TMB, 0.2 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.0), 20 min. (C) TEM image of MnO2 NSs in the presence of UA.

Effects of (A) time (50 μM MnO2 NSs, 10 μM UA, 0.5 mM TMB, 0.2 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.0)); (B) pH value (50 μM MnO2 NSs, 10 μM UA, 0.5 mM TMB, 20 min); (C) MnO2 NSs (10 μM UA, 0.5 mM TMB, 0.2 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.0), 20 min) and (D) TMB concentration (50 μM MnO2 NSs, 10 μM UA, 0.2 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.0), 20 min) on absorbance response.

(A) UA concentration-dependent absorption spectra. Inset shows the corresponding images. (B) The relationship between ΔA and UA concentration. Experimental conditions: 50 μM MnO2 NSs, 0.5 mM TMB, 0.2 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.0), 20 min.

Selectivity of this method for UA detection. The concentrations of UA, AA (a) and DA (b) are all 20 μM. The concentration of other interfering substances, including His (c), Ala (d), Gly (e), Glu (f), Ser (g), Thr (h), Asp (i), Ile (j), Leu (k), Pro (l), Arg (p), Urea (q), Mg2+ (r) and K+ (s), is 1000 μM. Experimental conditions: 50 μM MnO2 NSs, 0.5 mM TMB, 0.2 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 3.0), 20 min.
The principle of UA detection by enzyme free colorimetry.
Analytical performance of different UA detection methods.
Determination results of UA in real samples.

head.ce:title: Enzyme-free colorimetric detection of uric acid on the basis of MnO2 nanosheets - mediated oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′- tetramethylbenzidine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) were prepared and used as both oxidant of 3, 3′, 5, 5′- tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and recognizer of UA, a simple colorimetric approach for sensitive detection of uric acid (UA) was established. In the medium of weak acid, MnO2 NSs exhibited strong oxidation capacity and could oxidize colorless TMB to generate a blue oxidation product (oxTMB) with an absorbance signal. However, the introduction of UA could inhibit MnO2 NSs - mediated TMB oxidation and reduce the formation of oxTMB, resulting in the blue fading and the absorbance weakening. Under the conditions of incubation time 20 min, pH 3.0, TMB 0.5 mM, and MnO2 NSs 50 μM, the decrease in absorbance was linearly dependent on UA concentration in the range of 0.5–30.0 μM with a detection limit of 0.21 μM. Furthermore, the approach has been applied for determining UA in human urine samples with recoveries between 93.1 and 102.4 % and relative standard deviation less than 3.0 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (2. 6. 8 - trihydroxypurine, UA) is the final product of purine metabolism and an important marker biomolecule in human serum and urine [1]. Many studies suggested that the abnormal level of human UA is closely associated with a series of diseases such as gout, cardiovascular disease, hypertension, urolithiasis, and Lesch - Nyhan syndrome [2–4]. As a consequence, the accurate monitoring of UA content in serum and urine is of great significance for disease diagnosis. Multiple analysis strategies, such as electrochemistry [5], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [6], high - performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], chemiluminescence (CL) [8], fluorescence [9] and visible spectrophotometry [10], have been reported for determination of UA in body fluid. It is undeniable that these methods have achieved satisfactory results, but they also have their own limitations. Colorimetry is the most common visible spectrophotometry, which can be used to determine analytes with the help of visible spectrophotometer or naked eyes, and has the advantages of simplicity and practicality. The traditional colorimetric detection of UA is generally achieved by coupling uric acid oxidase (UAO) - catalyzed UA oxidation with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) - catalyzed 3, 3′, 5, 5′ - tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidation. Although this method has the advantages of simple instrument, easy operation, good selectivity and high accuracy, the use of two natural enzymes is its limitation due to the high price and environmental sensitivity of natural enzyme. In contrast to natural enzyme, artificial enzyme has the advantages of good stability, simple preparation and low price. In recent years, a few artificial enzymes [11,12] have been explored as an alternative of HRP. With the development of nanotechnology, a variety of nanomaterials with peroxidase mimetic activity have been utilized for colorimetric detection of UA in body fluids with satisfactory results [13,14].

MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) are typical two-dimensional nanomaterial with good photophysical properties, such as wide absorption range and big molar extinction coefficient (9.6 × 103 M−1 cm−1 at 380 nm [15]). As an effective fluorescence quencher or energy acceptor, MnO2 NSs have been widely applied for construction of different fluorescence sensing platforms [16–18]. Due to the electrode potential of 1.23 V vs NHE for MnO2/Mn2+ [19], MnO2 NSs show strong oxidation capacity. It can directly oxidize colorless TMB into blue oxTMB [20,21] with a molar extinction coefficient of 3.9 × 104 M−1 cm−1 [22]. However, the presence of some reducing substances can inhibit the color reaction between MnO2 NSs and TMB by decomposing MnO2 NSs. Based on these, a series of colorimetric strategies have been developed for sensitive detection of reducing analytes. For example, zhao et al. developed a colorimetric method to determine the activities of tyrosinase and catalase based on the substrate-triggered decomposition of MnO2 NSs [23]. Zhu et al. constructed a colorimetric sensing platform based on MnO2 NSs - TMB - NAD+/NADH for the quantitative detection of ethanol and pyruvic acid [24]. Besides, MnO2 NSs are also applied for colorimetric detection of oxalic acid [15], dopamine [25], glutathione [20], ascorbic acid [26], ascorbic acid-related alkaline phosphatase [27] and acetylcholinesterase activity [28], etc. The electrode potential of UA is in the range of 0.26 to 0.59 V [29], which is less than the electrode potential of 1.23 V for MnO2/Mn2+. Obviously, MnO2 NSs can be reduced by UA. Based on the facts that UA can inhibit MnO2 NSs from oxidizing I– ions to generate I2, thereby inhibiting I2 from etching gold nanorods, we developed an enzyme-free colorimetric technique to determine UA [29]. So far, the UA enzyme-free colorimetric detection is mainly based on the sensing strategy that the presence of UA causes the shifting of surface plasmon resonance absorption peak of gold nanorods [29] or silver nanoparticles [30]. However, gold and silver are precious metals, and the associated reagents are expensive. Here, we report a low-cost colorimetric approach for detecting UA without using any natural or artificial enzymes. Its detection principle is based on absorbance change rather than wavelength shift. As elucidated in Scheme 1 , in the absence of UA, MnO2 NSs oxidize colorless TMB to form blue oxidation product (oxTMB) with a characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm. In the presence of UA, MnO2 NSs are reduced to Mn2+ ions, the oxidation of TMB is then suppressed, causing a reduced absorbance of oxTMB. Thus, the UA concentration can be measured by monitoring the absorbance decrease of oxTMB. To our knowledge, this is the first report on enzyme-free assay of UA with TMB as the chromogenic substrate.

UA and TMB were bought from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid (CH3COOH), ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2⋅4H2O), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), urea, and tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH) were purchased from Da Mao Chemical Reagent (Tianjin, China). Alanine (Ala), arginine (Arg), aspartic acid (Asp), glucose (Glu), glycine (Gly), histidine (His), isoleucine (Ile), leucine (Leu), proline (Pro), serine (Ser), and threonine (Thr) were supplied by Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., ltd. (Guangdong, China). All chemicals were used without further purification, and all water used in the experiment is double deionized water (DDW).

All absorbance spectra were recorded on a Unico-4802 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained with a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer. The morphology of MnO2 NSs was identified by a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscopy (TEM). X‐Ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was determined on a D8 ADVANCE instrument. X‐Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on a K-Alpha spectrometer. The centrifugation was conducted on an H1850 high speed centrifuge.

MnO2 NSs were prepared in accordance with the literature [29]. 20 mL aqueous solution containing TMA⋅OH (0.6 M) and H2O2 (3 %) was prepared, which was then poured into 10 mL of MnCl2 solution (0.3 M) under agitation to obtain a dark brown solution. Afterwards, the resultant solution was continuously agitated at room temperature for 12 h. Finally, the pure MnO2 NSs were acquired after centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min) and washing (with DDW and methanol for 3 times separately). To prepare homogeneous MnO2 NSs stock solution, the obtained MnO2 NSs were re-dispersed in DDW following by sonification for 5 h. Given the molar extinction coefficient of 9.6 × 103 M -1cm−1 (380 nm) [15], the exact concentration of MnO2 NSs was calculated using the Lambert-Beer law.

500 μL of MnO2 NSs (200 μM), 200 μL of UA standard solution with different concentration, and 200 μL of TMB (5 mM) were fully mixed in a 2.0 mL centrifuge tube containing 1.1 mL of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 3.0, 0.2 M). The resultant solution (total volume 2.0 mL) was then incubated at room temperature for 20 min before being used for absorption spectrum measurement. The reduction of absorbance at 652 nm expressed as ΔA (ΔA = A 0- A, where A 0 and A are the absorbance of the detection system in the absence and presence of UA respectively) was applied to optimize the measurement conditions and fabricate calibration curve. All experiments were performed three times in parallel.

To characterize MnO2 NSs, UV–vis absorption spectrum, FTIR, XPS, XRD and TEM were adopted. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the colorless MnCl2 solution gives no absorption band in the scanning range. The brown MnO2 NSs solution exhibits a wide absorption band in the range of 300–700 nm with a characteristic absorption peak at 380 nm, which is caused by d-d transition of MnO6 octahedron in MnO2 lattice [31]. The emergence of stretching vibration of Mn–O in MnO6 octahedron framework at 513 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1B) indirectly confirms the oxidation of Mn2+ to Mn4+ and the formation of MnO2 NSs. The two absorption peaks at 3400 and 1620 cm−1 are most likely from the interlayer water in MnO2 NSs [32]. To further confirm the formation of MnO2 NSs, XPS and XRD measurements were performed. As exhibited in Fig. 1C, the existence of Mn and O elements can be proved in XPS spectrum of MnO2 NSs. The Mn2p spectrum (Fig. 1D) shows two characteristic binding energy values of 641.88 and 653.78 eV, which correspond to Mn2p3/2 and Mn2P1/2 spin–orbit peaks of MnO2 [33], respectively. By carrying out a peak-fitting deconvolution, the two peaks of Mn2p spectrum were separated into four peaks. The two peaks located at 643.28 and 654.28 eV are ascribed to Mn4+, and the peaks of 641.68 and 653.38 eV are fitted into Mn4+ near the oxygen vacancy [34]. Indicating the typical characteristic of Mn4+ oxidation state. In Fig. 1E, the XRD pattern exhibits three diffraction peaks centered at 18.8°, 36.6°, and 65.4°, which are in accord with the characteristic peaks of δ-MnO2 (JCPDS 18–0802) [35]. As seen in TEM image (Fig. 1F), the obtained MnO2 NSs display a two-dimensional and ultra-thin lamellar morphology, which is in agreement with that reported in literature [36]. All these confirm the successful preparation of MnO2 NSs. This sheet-like nanostructure can provide a large interface for the full reaction of MnO2 with reducing substances such as TMB and UA.

For evaluating the feasibility of colorimetric detection of UA, a series of control experiments were performed using TMB in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 3.0). As shown in Fig. 2 A, the colorless TMB solution gives no absorption band in the range of 550–800 nm. However, TMB containing MnO2 NSs results in a strong characteristic absorption peak at 652 nm with the color conversion from colorless to blue, due to the generation of oxTMB. After adding UA to TMB containing MnO2 NSs, the absorbance of oxTMB decreases and the color of solution becomes lighter. Suggesting that the generation of oxTMB is reduced. Low concentration of UA (10 μM) results in a small decrease of absorbance and less obvious fading. While the high concentration of UA (20 μM) causes a significant decrease of absorbance and a significant shallowing of the solution. The color and absorbance of TMB solution do not change before and after adding Mn2+ ions as displayed in Fig. 2A, indicating that Mn2+ ions cannot oxidize colorless TMB to blue oxTMB. These results demonstrate that UA can inhibit MnO2 NSs - mediated TMB oxidation and lead to a decrease of absorbance, and the decreased value is correlated with UA concentration. Thus, we can measure UA by the decrease of absorbance.

To elucidate the principle of UA inhibiting TMB oxidation by MnO2 NSs, the absorption spectra and TEM images of MnO2 NSs with and without UA were recorded. As displayed in Fig. 2B, the absorption peak at 380 nm weakens or even disappears after adding to MnO2 NSs. Compared with the original morphology of MnO2 NSs (Fig. 1F), the morphology of MnO2 NSs in the presence of UA is fragmented (Fig. 2C). All these imply that the MnO2 have be reduced to Mn2+ by UA. Therefore, it can be concluded that UA causes the decomposition of MnO2, thereby inhibiting the conversion of TMB to oxTMB. Although the addition of UA can also reduce the absorbance of MnO2 NSs solution, the molar extinction coefficient of MnO2 NSs (9.6 × 103 M−1 cm−1 at 380 nm [15]) is smaller than that of oxTMB (3.9 × 104 M−1 cm−1 at 652 nm [22]), and the absorbance decreasing caused by UA to MnO2 NSs solution is lower than that to TMB solution containing MnO2 NSs. Considering the sensitivity of the method, TMB was used as the chromogenic substrate.

Several factors, including incubation time, pH value, the concentrations of TMB and MnO2 NSs, which may influence absorbance response, were optimized by using ΔA as optimization criterion. The effect of incubation time on ΔA was examined in the range of 10 to 35 min. As shown in Fig. 3 A, an increase in incubation time from 10 to 20 min results in the increased ΔA while a further increase in incubation time from 20 to 35 min leads to a gradual decrease. Thus, the optimum incubation time was 20 min. Since the conversion of TMB is usually carried out in acidic environment [37], the effect of pH ranging from 2.5 to 5.0 was studied in HAc-NaAc buffer. As shown in Fig. 3B, the ΔA reaches the maximum at pH 3.0, and beyond this value, ΔA decreases instead. Therefore, subsequent experiments were performed in buffer at pH 3.0. The influences of TMB and MnO2 NSs concentration on ΔA were also evaluated. As exhibited in Fig. 3C and D. the maximum ΔA is obtained at 0.5 mM TMB and 50 μM MnO2 NSs, which were selected for the further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear response range and detection limit
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, UA concentration-dependent absorption spectra were recorded. As displayed in Fig. 4 A, the absorbance at 652 nm continuously declines when UA concentration increases from 0 to 50 μM, and the gradual fading of solution color is also visible to the naked eye (inset in Fig. 4A). ΔA increases with the increase of UA concentration as displayed Fig. 4B, and the curve deviates from the straight line is observed when the UA concentration exceeds 30 μM. Consequently, a desirable linear relationship, ΔA = 0.0425C UA + 0.0282, with UA concentration ranging from 0.5 ∼ 30 μM (R 2 = 0.9956) is presented in the inset in Fig. 4B. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.21 μΜ based on 3σ/slope rule, showing high sensitivity for UA determination. The relative standard deviations (RSD) for five replicate detection of 2.5 μM、10 μM、20 μM and 30 μM UA are 2.1 %, 1.4 %, 0.8 %, and 0.6 % respectively, suggesting a good precision of this approach. The detection of UA based on MnO2 NSs -TMB shows more favorable analytical performance in linear range or LOD than the other UA determination approaches reported previously (Table 1 ). More importantly, the preparation of MnO2 NSs does not need any noble metal or heavy metal, and no enzyme (including natural enzyme and mimic enzyme) is used in this method. Hence, the developed method has the advantages of high sensitivity, low cost and environmental friendliness, making it a promising method for UA analysis.

To verify the selectivity of the method, the influence of the common species in human urine, such as amino acids, reducing biomolecules, and glucose, etc., on UA detection was investigated. Fig. 5 exhibits the ΔA of MnO2 NSs -TMB upon UA in the background of above potential coexisting species. Although the concentration of amino acids, urea, glucose, Mg2+ and K+ ions is 50 times that of UA, no obvious absorbance responses are observed. AA and DA interfere with the detection of UA. However, the normal content of UA (1.4 ∼ 4.5 mM [14]) in human urine is much higher than that of AA and DA [41–43], their existence has little influence on UA detection. These results confirm that the proposed method has good selectivity and can be used for detecting UA.

To verify its availability, the developed method was applied for determining UA in human urine samples which were provided by healthy volunteers in our laboratory. To avoid the interference from sample matrix as much as possible, the urine sample was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min), and the supernatant was diluted 20 times with HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 3.0). 100 μL of diluted sample was taken and determined according to the procedure described in 2.3. As shown in Table 2 , the concentration of UA in the two test solutions is 9.12 and 7.71 μM. In consideration of the sample dilution (400 times) caused by the analysis process, the UA concentration in the original urine samples is 3648 μM and 3084 μM respectively, within the normal range of 1.4 to 4.5 mM [14]. Moreover, the recovery of UA in urine samples is between 93.1 % and 102.4 % with RSD less than 3.0 %. Demonstrating that the proposed method shows good accuracy and precision when used to measure UA in real samples.

In this work, TMB acts as chromogenic substrate, and MnO2 NSs are used as both oxidant of TMB and recognizer of UA. When UA is absent, MnO2 NSs are all used to oxidize TMB, causing high absorbance of the solution. When UA is present, part of MnO2 NSs are decomposed, resulting in decreases of oxTMB and absorbance. Since the decrease in absorbance is associated with UA concentration, a simple colorimetric method has been established to sensitively determine UA. Under the chosen conditions, the method exhibits good response toward UA with a linear range from 0.5 to 30 μM. More importantly, the proposed method shows its potential application in determining UA in urine samples. Compared with other colorimetric methods for the detection of UA, this method has the advantage of not requiring any natural or artificial enzymes. The research not only provides a new strategy for colorimetric sensing of UA, but also expands the application range of MnO2 NSs. The limitation of the method is that it is not suitable for continuous and in situ analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the opening project of Guangxi key laboratory of electrochemical energy materials (KF2020012) for its financial support.
